params.text:
The nine protected characteristics

The Equality Act 2010 makes it unlawful to discriminate against someone because of any of these nine protected characteristics:

  • Age – being a particular age or age group.
  • Disability – a physical or mental impairment with a long-term effect on everyday activities.
  • Gender reassignment – proposing to undergo, undergoing or having undergone a process to reassign one's sex.
  • Marriage and civil partnership – being married or in a civil partnership.
  • Pregnancy and maternity – being pregnant or having recently given birth.
  • Race – including colour, nationality, and ethnic or national origin.
  • Religion or belief – any religion, or a philosophical belief, including having no religion.
  • Sex – being a man or a woman.
  • Sexual orientation – being attracted to the same sex, the opposite sex, or both.

The people you support – and your colleagues – are all protected under these characteristics.

params.question: Which of the following is one of the nine protected characteristics under the Equality Act 2010?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Gender reassignment is one of the nine protected characteristics.
This is not a protected characteristic in its own right, although race (which includes national origin) is.
Income and social class are not protected characteristics under the Equality Act 2010.
Political opinion is not a protected characteristic under the Act, though a philosophical belief may be covered under 'religion or belief'.
